Group of monument, Royal Palace Mandu
Cave, Temple and Inscriptions, Junaar, Lenyadri
Ancient Remains on both Udaigiri & Khandagiri Hills
Marble Pavillion and balustrade on the Ana Sagar bund and ruins of the marble Hammam Behind the Ana sagar Bund

Taj Mahal.title: Taj Mahal
Taj Mahal.description: The ultimate monument to love, constructed of ivory-white marble on the south bank of the Yamuna River. It is a UNESCO World Heritage site and one of the New Seven Wonders of the World.
field_notes: Dominates both domestic and foreign charts; holds 40M+ visitors across the decade.
Winter peak is extreme: December sees up to 4x the visitor numbers of June.
Domestic visitors comprise 90%+ of total footfall, but it remains the top draw for foreign tourists.

Qutub Minar.title: Qutub Minar
Qutub Minar.description: A 73-meter tall minaret that forms part of the Qutb complex, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Built of red sandstone and marble, featuring intricate carvings and verses from the Quran.
field_notes: Consistently ranks 2nd or 3rd nationally, showing massive, steady domestic interest.
The iron pillar in the complex is a metallurgy marvel, remaining rust-free for over 1,600 years.
Accessibility in Delhi drives steady year-round traffic compared to remote historical sites.

Red Fort.title: Red Fort (Lal Qila)
Red Fort.description: A historic fort built of red sandstone that served as the main residence of the Mughal Emperors for nearly 200 years. Represents the zenith of Mughal creativity.
field_notes: Shows massive spikes around national holidays (Independence Day and Republic Day).
Agra Fort and Red Fort show similar architectural heritage and highly correlated visitor counts.
Suffers slightly higher traffic congestion in peak months due to its location in Old Delhi.

Sun Temple, Konark.title: Sun Temple, Konark
Sun Temple, Konark.description: A monumental temple dedicated to the Sun God Surya, designed as a colossal chariot with 24 wheels drawn by seven horses. Famous for its stone carvings and architectural precision.
field_notes: A massive outlier in the eastern region, drawing visitors in numbers comparable to Agra and Delhi.
Peak visits cluster around winter, but coast-adjacent location helps support shoulder seasons.
High domestic visitor concentration, reflecting its status as a pilgrimage and heritage site.

Golconda Fort.title: Golconda Fort
Golconda Fort.description: A fortified citadel and former capital of the Qutb Shahi kingdom. Renowned for its acoustics (a clap at the gates can be heard at the top of the hill), diamond mines (Kollur mine), and massive walls.
field_notes: Key pillar of Hyderabad's high-density circle tourism.
Acquisition of visitors has grown at a steady 5% CAGR pre-2019, primarily domestic driven.
Acoustic design features and light shows make it a highly interactive tourist site.

Ellora Caves.title: Ellora Caves
Ellora Caves.description: One of the largest rock-cut monastery-temple cave complexes in the world, featuring Buddhist, Hindu, and Jain monuments. Highlights include the spectacular Kailash Temple (Cave 16), carved from a single rock.
field_notes: Aurangabad's primary star, drawing millions of travelers to central India.
Foreign visitor percentage is higher here than the national average, driven by international archaeological interest.
Kailash Temple's sheer scale represents the zenith of monolithic rock-cut engineering.

Charminar.title: Charminar
Charminar.description: A landmark mosque and monument featuring four grand arches and minarets, built to celebrate the end of a plague. Situated in the heart of Hyderabad's old city.
field_notes: Shows massive localized street traffic and evening visitors, which is partly under-captured in official daytime tickets.
Perfect 50/50 balance between historical monument and active urban marketplace.
Extremely high density of domestic visitors; very compact physical footprint compared to sprawling forts.

Mamallapuram.title: Group of Monuments Mamallapuram
Mamallapuram.description: A collection of 7th- and 8th-century sanctuary monuments on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal. Famous for the Shore Temple, Pancha Rathas, and Descent of the Ganges rock relief.
field_notes: A major coastal site that anchors southern India's cultural tourism loop.
A strong foreign favorite, ranking very high in international visitor counts due to beach-heritage combination.
Open-air seaside layout creates unique conservation challenges but provides scenic appeal.
